reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.
reviews.strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.
reviews.questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?
reviews.limitations: - Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.

Strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.

Weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.

Questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?

Limitations:
- Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
This paper proposes two new metrics allowing to measure and explain the diversity of a generated set of images w.r.t a training set. Instead of the usual distributional distances relying upon an embedding space from a pre-trained model, these metrics rely on a set of textual attributes. The similarity between an image and an attribute is computed using their representation in a common semantic space, via the CLIP model - vectors are shifted using a centre of training images/attributes to make similarity scores more meaningful. Several ways to obtain attributes (Captioning, User-based or GPT-based) are investigated. The usefulness of the metrics is tested with an experiment injecting images correlated with target attributes in data, an experiment aiming to detect a specific attribute relationship in a curated dataset, and in a comparison of several generative models.

Strengths:
- This paper proposes an interesting approach, aiming at using the common semantic space between images and text proposed by CLIP to measure attribute and image relatedness, in order to provide interpretable representations of generated images and measure attribute-based metrics between a reference and generated dataset; such an application seems relatively original to me. 
- The two first experiments demonstrate well the usefulness of the approach, able to detect a shift when the data has been curated by human, based on which attributes.

Weaknesses:
- The presentation of the paper could be improved upon. This include the writing, and the readability of the figures, as well as the quantity of information provided. This last points concerns mainly the presentation and framing of the problem of interpretability of representations, as well as the presentation and motivation of the experimental settings. 
- In particular, the paper lacks related work on concept-based representation for interpretability of images. While building metrics dedicated to generative model seems new to me, there are based on an idea which has been explored extensively before. See for example "Concept Whitening for Interpretable Image Recognition, Chen et al, 2020". 
- The paper focuses on a narrow choice of methods to generate attributes, which, to me, should be one of the key experimental investigation of the paper. Notably, the previous literature explores using different kind of attributes, coming from existing data (for example, "Interpretable Basis Decomposition for Visual Explanation, Zhou et al, 2018") or to be learnt ("A Framework to Learn with Interpretation", Parekh et al, 2021). The authors only (very shortly) argue about the number of needed attributes.
- The toy experiments seem relevant but are very fastly presented and should be expanded upon. The remaining experiments are too short to be convincing and only focus on a handful of models.

Questions:
- Could you better formalize the notation $DCS_{\mathcal{X}}(a_{i,j})$ for joint distributions ?
- Is it necessary to center the text and images differently ? They lie in the same semantic space. Also, would it not be reasonable to be using the attributes themselves, rather than captions ?

Limitations:
- Previous distributional metrics are several times referred as relying on external models in your paper. However, the attributes that you use also rely on external models (except the USER one, of course - but in this case, the computing of DCS still relies on a captioning model). How would you address this issue ?

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.
reviews.strengths: The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.
reviews.weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.
reviews.questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.
reviews.limitations: It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.

Strengths:
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.

Weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that.

In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. reviews.text:
**Reviewer #X**

**Overall Impression:**
This paper introduces a novel and timely approach to evaluating generative models, moving beyond traditional metrics like FID to provide more interpretable insights into generator performance. The authors propose the Directional CLIP Score (DCS) and two new metrics, Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD). These metrics aim to quantify attribute prominence and assess the alignment of attribute distributions and relationships between generated images and training data, leveraging the CLIP embedding space. The effort to provide explainable evaluations, offering insights into diversity and potential biases, is a significant contribution towards trustworthy AI, especially considering current issues observed in models like Stable Diffusion. While the proposed methodology is sound and addresses a critical need for interpretable metrics (as existing metrics like FID rely on classification-trained embeddings which may not fully capture generative model performance), the paper raises several concerns that need to be addressed for clarity and robustness.

**Strengths:**

*   **Novelty in Evaluation Perspective:** The paper makes a commendable effort to evaluate generative models from multiple, interpretable perspectives. The introduction of DCS, SaKLD, and PaKLD provides a fresh approach to understanding what underlying factors contribute to a generative model's performance.
*   **Contribution to Trustworthy AI:** The proposed metrics are highly beneficial for the field of trustworthy AI. By offering explainable evaluations that can highlight diversity and biases in generated images, they provide crucial tools for auditing and improving generative models, directly addressing contemporary issues like bias in large-scale models.
*   **Addressing Interpretability Gap:** The paper correctly identifies a critical need for new evaluation metrics that can interpret the underlying factors of generative model performance, moving beyond the limitations of metrics like FID which use embedding spaces (e.g., Inception V3) primarily trained for classification tasks.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**

While the core idea is strong, several aspects require clarification and further discussion:

1.  **Metric Stability and Sample Size:** A notable practical limitation is that the proposed metrics require a significant number of samples (50,000) to achieve stable results. While the experiments show stability with this number, this is an intrinsic limitation that should be explicitly discussed in terms of its implications for research and deployment, as generating and evaluating such large datasets can be computationally intensive.
2.  **Novelty of CLIP Embedding Use & Explainable Evaluation:** The paper's novelty regarding the use of CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative models (lines 96-97) could be more clearly differentiated from previously proposed methods. For instance, clarifying differences from works like Kynkäänniemi et al. [18] would strengthen the claim of novelty. Furthermore, it is unclear if this paper is the first to propose the concept of "explainable evaluation" for generative models; a more thorough review of prior works in this specific area, if applicable, would be beneficial.
3.  **Potential Biases in CLIP Embedding Space:** A major methodological concern is the reliance on the CLIP embedding space itself, which may harbor inherent biases (e.g., high correlation between "female" and "makeup" in the embedding space). Such biases could potentially affect the accuracy and fairness of the CLIP score or DCS. The authors should address this concern directly and discuss their perspective on how these potential biases in the underlying embedding space might impact the proposed metrics.
4.  **DCS Computation and Data Sharing:** The method of computing the center of training images (CX) for Directional CLIP Score (DCS) raises questions about whether all training images need to be shared for evaluation. This has significant practical and privacy implications. Clarification on whether sharing all training images is a prerequisite for using DCS would be crucial.
5.  **Justification for BLIP Integration:** The paper notes that an auxiliary image captioning model, BLIP, is used to define the center of image captions (CT) for DCS. However, the improvement in performance gained by introducing BLIP seems minimal. The authors should justify if this performance improvement is truly worth the added complexity and computational trade-off introduced by integrating an additional large model.
6.  **Clarity in Experimental Details (Table 5):**
    *   Table 5 includes a column labeled "FID_clip," which lacks an explanation. A clear definition of what "FID_clip" represents (e.g., FID calculated using CLIP embeddings instead of Inception V3) is needed.
    *   The computation method for SaKLD for a single attribute in Table 5 is not clearly explained. Providing a step-by-step breakdown or a more detailed description of this calculation would enhance clarity.
7.  **Interpretation of Scores:** While the metrics are proposed to be interpretable, the paper could provide more insights or guidance on how to interpret the numerical values of the proposed SaKLD and PaKLD scores. What constitutes a "good" or "bad" score, and how should one practically use these numbers to understand generator performance?
8.  **Extension of PaKLD:** The paper proposes PaKLD for paired attribute relationships. It would be valuable to discuss the possibility of extending PaKLD to evaluate relationships among more than two attributes, exploring the scalability and feasibility of such an extension.

**Technical Details Addressed:**
The paper correctly identifies FID's reliance on Inception V3. It introduces DCS using CLIP, calculating similarity between directions from CX (center of training images) and CT (center of image captions defined by BLIP). SaKLD compares attribute distributions (PDFs of DCS values) via KL divergence, while PaKLD extends this to joint probability density distributions for attribute pairs. The use of Gaussian kernel density estimation and sampling 10,000 points from PDFs, along with N=20 attributes, are noted. The mention of "FID_clip" in Table 5 suggests an alternative FID variant, which needs further explanation.

**Recommendation:**
This paper presents a technically solid and innovative approach to addressing a critical gap in generative model evaluation. The proposed metrics (DCS, SaKLD, PaKLD) offer a promising path towards more interpretable and trustworthy AI. While the identified weaknesses are significant and require thorough addressing, they primarily pertain to clarification, discussion, and justification rather than fundamental flaws in the methodology. With careful revisions incorporating the suggestions above, particularly regarding novelty claims, potential biases, practical implementation details, and clarity in experimental presentation, this paper would make a valuable contribution to the field. Therefore, I recommend a **Weak Accept**.

If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.

Limitations:
It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

extracted_ideas.summary: The review acknowledges the paper's novel approach to evaluating generative models, which introduces Directional CLIP Score (DCS) and two new metrics, Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD), to provide interpretable insights into generator performance beyond traditional FID. While appreciating the contribution to trustworthy AI, the reviewer raises several concerns regarding the metrics' stability, novelty, potential biases in the embedding space, and practical implementation details.
strengths: The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics is considered novel and a contribution.
The proposal of these metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI, especially considering current issues like bias in stable diffusion.
weaknesses: A significant number of samples (50,000) are still required to obtain stable results for the proposed metrics, which is an intrinsic limitation.
The paper's novelty regarding the use of CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative models (lines 96-97) needs clearer differentiation from previously proposed methods.
It is unclear if this paper is the first to propose the concept of "explainable evaluation" for generative models, and further previous works might need to be cited.

A major concern is the reliance on the embedding space, specifically the potential for biases within the CLIP space itself (e.g., high correlation between "female" and "makeup"), which could affect the accuracy of CLIP score or DCS.
The method of computing the center of training images for Directional CLIP Score (DCS) raises questions about whether all training images need to be shared for evaluation.
The improvement in performance from introducing the auxiliary image captioning model (BLIP) for DCS seems minimal, questioning if it is worth the trade-off.
Table 5 lacks an explanation for the "FID_clip" column.
The computation method for SaKLD for a single attribute in Table 5 is not clearly explained.
suggestions: Clarify the differences and novelty of using the CLIP embedding space for generative model evaluation compared to previously proposed methods (e.g., Kynkäänniemi et al. [18]).
Determine whether this paper is the first to introduce "explainable evaluation" for generative models and share any additional previous works if applicable.
Address the concern about potential biases in the CLIP embedding space itself and discuss the authors' perspective on this issue.
Clarify whether sharing all training images is necessary for evaluating a generative model using Directional CLIP Score (DCS).
Justify if the performance improvement gained by introducing the auxiliary BLIP model for DCS is worth the trade-off.
Provide an explanation for the "FID_clip" column in Table 5.
Explain how SaKLD is computed for a single attribute, especially in the context of Table 5.
Discuss the possibility of extending PaKLD to evaluate relationships among more than two attributes.
Provide insights or guidance on how to interpret the numerical values of the proposed SaKLD and PaKLD scores.
main_points: Existing generative model evaluation metrics, such as FID, rely on embedding spaces (e.g., Inception V3) trained for classification, which may not effectively capture features relevant for generative model evaluation and lack interpretability.
There is a critical need for new evaluation metrics that can interpret the underlying factors contributing to a generative model's performance.
The paper introduces Directional CLIP Score (DCS) as a novel method to create an interpretable embedding space based on attribute prominence, utilizing a pre-trained CLIP model.

Two new evaluation metrics, Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD), are proposed to measure the alignment of single attribute distributions and relationships between multiple attributes, respectively.
The proposed metrics aim to provide explainable evaluations, offering insights into diversity and biases in generated images, which is beneficial for trustworthy AI.
A key limitation is the requirement for a significant number of samples (50,000) to achieve stable results with the proposed metrics.
Concerns exist regarding the novelty of using CLIP for evaluation and the concept of explainable metrics, necessitating clearer distinctions from prior work.
The potential for inherent biases within the CLIP embedding space itself (e.g., attribute correlations) is a significant methodological concern.
Practical considerations, such as the necessity of sharing training data for DCS calculation and the cost-benefit of integrating BLIP, require further clarification.
technical_details: FID is calculated using embeddings from Inception V3, which is trained for image classification.
The paper introduces Directional CLIP Score (DCS) as a method for quantifying attribute prominence in an image.
DCS uses a pre-trained CLIP model as its embedding space.
DCS calculates similarity between directions derived from the center of training images (CX) and the center of image captions (CT) in the CLIP embedding space.
An auxiliary image captioning model, BLIP, is used to define CT for DCS.
Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) compares attribute distributions (PDFs of DCS values) between training data and generated images using Kullback-Leibler Divergence.
Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) assesses the preservation of attribute relationships by comparing joint probability density distributions of attribute pairs using KL divergence.
Experiments use Gaussian kernel density estimation to estimate PDFs of DCS and sample 10,000 points from each PDF.
The proposed metrics show stability when using more than 50,000 images.
The number of attributes used in experiments is N=20.
The review mentions 'FID_clip' in Table 5, indicating another FID variant possibly using CLIP embeddings.

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.
reviews.strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.
reviews.questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.
reviews.limitations: see weakness

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
**Review Summary and Recommendation:**

This paper introduces a novel and meaningful approach to evaluating the quality of generative models by leveraging attribute information from training data. The core idea of using attribute-level insights to understand data distribution imitation and pinpoint areas of poor performance is highly interesting. The proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC) is a concise and technically sound contribution, which forms the basis for two new attribute-specific metrics: Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD). While the conceptual framework is strong, the experimental validation currently lacks the quantitative rigor needed to definitively establish the superiority of these new metrics over existing ones.

Given the innovative nature of the core idea and the elegance of the Directional CLIPScore, the paper holds significant promise. However, the identified weaknesses regarding experimental validation and potential biases in attribute extraction warrant careful consideration. Therefore, I recommend a **Borderline Accept**. Addressing the suggestions below would substantially strengthen the manuscript.

**Strengths:**

*   **Novelty and Significance:** The central research question—utilizing dataset attribute information for evaluating generative model quality—is both novel and highly meaningful. This approach offers valuable insights into how well models capture specific data characteristics, moving beyond aggregated scores.
*   **Technical Contribution:** The proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC) is well-conceived. It offers an elegant solution to limitations of direct CLIPScore and appears effective in the presented case studies, serving as a robust foundation for the subsequent attribute-based metrics.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Attribute Extraction Bias:** The reliance on a GPT-based method for attribute extraction (Section 3.3) raises concerns. If the generated list of attributes is not truly representative of the underlying data distribution, it could introduce biases that compromise the study's primary focus on correlating model performance with training data attributes. A more robust justification for the representativeness of the extracted attributes, or an analysis of the sensitivity of results to attribute selection, would be beneficial.
2.  **Experimental Validation:** The experimental validation presented in Sections 5.1-5.3, while showing consistency with expected design principles, does not provide strong quantitative evidence of the proposed metrics' superiority.
    *   **Suggestion 2a:** It is crucial to analyze and report the correlation coefficient between the proposed evaluation metrics (SaKLD, PaKLD) and human evaluation scores. This would provide direct evidence of their practical utility and alignment with human perception.
    *   **Suggestion 2b:** To demonstrate the advancement, the authors should explicitly show whether the proposed evaluation metrics achieve an improved correlation coefficient compared to previous, established evaluation indicators across relevant benchmarks. This comparative analysis is essential for convincing readers of their practical value.

**Technical Details and Minor Comments:**

1.  **CLIPScore Alternative:** In Section 3.1, the paper discusses limitations of CLIPScore and introduces DSC. Could the problem described (e.g., misdirection in similarity) be resolved by simply using the Euclidean distance between the embeddings `E(x)` and `E(a)` instead of the cosine similarity and subsequent directional adjustment? A brief discussion on why DSC is preferred over a distance-based approach would be helpful.
2.  **Notation Overload:** The variable 'N' appears to have different meanings in Equation (2) and Equation (5). Please clarify or use distinct notation to avoid confusion.

**Overall Assessment:**

The paper presents an exciting and potentially impactful direction for generative model evaluation. The core idea is strong, and the Directional CLIPScore is a solid technical contribution. However, the current experimental validation is descriptive rather than quantitatively comparative, which is a significant limitation for a new set of evaluation metrics. Addressing the suggestions, particularly those related to quantitative validation against human judgment and existing metrics, would significantly enhance the paper's impact and persuasiveness. The identified technical details are minor and easily fixable. With a focused revision addressing these points, this work has the potential to be a valuable contribution to the field.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

The paper introduces Directional CLIPScore (DSC) to address limitations of direct CLIPScore, and two new metrics, Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD), built upon DSC.
strengths: The research question of using dataset attribute information for model generation quality evaluation is very interesting and meaningful.
The proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC) is concise and appears effective in case studies.
weaknesses: The GPT-based attribute extraction method (Section 3.3) may introduce biases if the generated list is not representative, potentially affecting the study's focus on model-training data correlation.

The experimental validation (Sections 5.1-5.3) is not fully convincing, as it mainly shows consistency with expected designs rather than providing strong quantitative evidence that the proposed metrics outperform existing ones.
suggestions: Analyze the correlation coefficient between the proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation.
Demonstrate whether the proposed evaluation metrics show improvement in correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators.
main_points: The paper's core idea of using attribute information for generative model evaluation is novel and meaningful.
The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) is a well-conceived and effective technical contribution.
There is a concern about potential bias in the GPT-based attribute selection method, which might affect the representativeness of attributes used for evaluation.
The experimental validation lacks strong quantitative evidence to demonstrate the superiority of the proposed metrics over existing ones, specifically in terms of correlation with human evaluation.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.
reviews.strengths: The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise
reviews.questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?
reviews.limitations: limitations have been addressed
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
This paper introduces SaKLD and PaKLD, novel attribute-based interpretable evaluation metrics for generative models. These metrics leverage a "Directional CLIPScore" to quantify attribute distributions and correlations, aiming to provide explanations for model behavior beyond simple ranking. While the motivation for developing more interpretable evaluation methods is strong and the proposed idea has significant potential impact for improving the understanding and development of generative models, several fundamental concerns regarding the methodology, experimental validation, and clarity of technical details preclude acceptance at this stage.

**Strengths:**

*   The motivation behind creating interpretable evaluation metrics for generative models is excellent. There is a clear need for methods that can explain *why* a model performs well or poorly, rather than just providing a scalar score.
*   The idea of using attribute distributions and correlations to provide these explanations holds considerable potential for advancing the field of generative model evaluation.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Methodological and Experimental Inadequacy:** The overall methodology and the experimental results are not convincing enough to justify the broad adoption of the proposed metrics. The experiments are inadequate and do not sufficiently demonstrate how well SaKLD and PaKLD perform compared to previously proposed evaluation metrics. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is poorly supported by the experimental setup, with results often insufficiently explained.
*   **Limited Applicability and Generalizability:** The metrics heavily rely on attributes in the form of text descriptions. This dependency significantly limits their applicability and generalizability compared to existing metrics that evaluate quality directly from generated images, without requiring explicit attribute definitions.
*   **Limited Novelty:** The technical novelty of the proposed metrics appears limited. They primarily combine existing components, such as CLIPScore for generating interpretable embeddings and KL Divergence for comparing probability distributions, rather than introducing fundamentally new concepts.
*   **Ambiguity in Technical Details and Assumptions:** Several critical parts of the paper are ambiguous. For instance, the calculation method for the "center of text attributes" is unclear. The definition and calculation of "accuracy" in Table 1, including what constitutes "positive samples" and how "well they are aligned," are not adequately explained. Furthermore, the fundamental assumption in Line 196-197, stating that a good generative model should produce *exactly* the same quantity of each attribute as the training data, is questionable and lacks sufficient justification or proof. This assumption may be overly restrictive or inappropriate for evaluating generative models, which often aim for plausible variations rather than exact replication.
*   **Concerns with Embedding Space Compatibility:** The use of different text and image encoders (CLIP) raises concerns about the compatibility of their respective embedding spaces and the validity of calculating similarity between these embeddings. Their respective downstream tasks might influence these embeddings, and the paper does not sufficiently address whether such comparisons are appropriate.
*   **Unsupported Claims and Missing Evidence:** Diversity is cited as a main motivation for the metrics, yet theoretical or empirical work to support this claim is entirely missing. The claim in Line 56-58 also does not seem correct. Overall, the contributions do not appear significant due to insufficient validation to support the claims made.
*   **Presentation and Consistency Issues:** Figure 2 is never referred to in the text, suggesting it might be unnecessary. There is also an inconsistency between Line 253 and the Figure 4 caption regarding the composition of the generated set.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

*   **Strengthen Methodology and Experiments:** A significant overhaul of the methodology and a substantial expansion of the experimental validation are crucial. This should include more rigorous comparisons against a wider array of established generative model evaluation metrics, clearly demonstrating the advantages of SaKLD and PaKLD.
*   **Improve Literature Survey:** Expand the literature survey to cover more relevant prior work in the domain of generative modeling evaluation.
*   **Clarify Technical Details:**
    *   Provide a clear, step-by-step explanation of how the "center of text attributes" is calculated.
    *   Offer a comprehensive explanation of the 'accuracy' results in Table 1, defining "positive samples" and clarifying "how well they are aligned."
    *   Elaborate on how "each channel of the embedding" is utilized in Section 3.1.
    *   Address the concerns regarding the compatibility of embedding spaces from different CLIP encoders and justify the validity of calculating similarity between them.
*   **Justify Core Assumptions:** Provide strong theoretical or empirical justification for the assumption in Line 196-197 regarding the exact replication of attribute counts by generative models, or consider refining this assumption if it is indeed too restrictive.
*   **Address Presentation Issues:**
    *   Correct the typo in Line 43: change "Figure 1 (b)" to "Figure 1 (a)".
    *   Correct the heading capitalization in Line 168: "Section 3.3 Attribute Selection Methodologies".
    *   Improve wording in Line 56 for technical correctness, perhaps suggesting "if model lacks 'representation' of essential attributes."
    *   Address the missing link to the specified figure in Line 147.
    *   Either properly reference Figure 2 in the text and explain its purpose, or remove it if it is not essential.
    *   Resolve the inconsistency between Line 253 and the Figure 4 caption.

**Overall Assessment:**

While the paper presents an interesting and well-motivated idea with significant potential, the current presentation suffers from a lack of technical soundness, insufficient experimental validation, and ambiguities in key methodological details. These shortcomings prevent a clear demonstration of the proposed metrics' utility and superiority over existing methods. A substantial revision addressing the fundamental weaknesses and clarifying the technical aspects would be necessary to reconsider this work.

**Decision:** 4: Borderline reject
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.

Strengths:
The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.

Weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise

Questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?

Limitations:
limitations have been addressed

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Residual Scheduling: A New Reinforcement Learning Approach to Solving Job Shop Scheduling Problem

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based constructive heuristic to solve the (Flexible) Job Shop Scheduling Problem. An instance of the problem is represented as a graph and fed into a Graph Neural Network-based model which outputs a score for each candidate (operation-machine) pair. The model is trained with the REINFORCE algorithm with as baseline a classic Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic. The novelty of the paper lies in the update of the state after each action: the graph is updated by removing the operations which have already been executed to focus on the most relevant information, which is the residual operations and remaining times of the ongoing ones. The proposed approach is experimentally shown to outperform RL-based constructive heuristics on classic JSSP and FJSSP benchmarks.
reviews.strengths:
1. Sound and interesting idea of removing irrelevant information from the state 
1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.
1. The model was proposed for the JSSP and easily adapted to the Flexible JSSP
1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020
reviews.questions:
1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1. The paper claims: “Interestingly, using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for the collections with sizes larger than 100 … A strong implication is that our RS approach can be scaled up for job sizes and even reach the optimal for sufficient large job count.” Do you have an idea of why the model would work better on unseen large instances versus instances of the same size as the training ones? Does OR-tools return the optimal solutions for these larger instances? Can it be that the quality of the reference solutions decreases and therefore the “optimality” gap becomes smaller?
1. Are there stronger non-learning-based baselines for the JSPP other than the greedy PDR heuristics presented in Table 2? To be able to appreciate the performance of the proposed approach, it would be useful and more convincing to compare it to the best heuristics for this problem, beyond simple greedy ones. For example maybe [2]?
1. Appendix, Algorithm 1, Line 6:  to compute this makespan at state s_t, given action a_t, do you do a rollout of the current policy \pi_{\theta} with the updated parameter \theta? This would mean doing a rollout until the end of both the baseline and current policy at each step of the trajectory? How long did the training take?
1. How is the average computation time computed? (Table 13) In particular, was the policy applied to each instance individually or were instances batched? 
1. The discussion L322 about the time it takes for RS/L2D/ScheduleNet versus OR-Tools can be a bit misleading: OR-tools is an exact solver. If spends a lot of time proving the optimality of the solution. Probably it could return a good quality solution much faster if used as a heuristic. In addition it runs on CPUs and not GPUs therefore just comparing the time is not really fair. 

[2] CY Zhang, A tabu search algorithm with a new neighborhood structure for the job shop scheduling problem, Computers & Operations Research, 2007
reviews.limitations: Not explicitly addressed by the paper.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
